text: With the rapid generation of spatial transcriptomics (ST) data, integrative analysis of multiple ST datasets from different conditions, technologies and developmental stages is becoming increasingly important. Here we present a graph attention neural network called STAligner for integrating and aligning ST datasets, enabling spatially aware data integration, simultaneous spatial domain identification and downstream comparative analysis. We apply STAligner to ST datasets of the human cortex slices from different samples, the mouse olfactory bulb slices generated by two profiling technologies, the mouse hippocampus tissue slices under normal and Alzheimer's disease conditions, and the spatiotemporal atlases of mouse organogenesis. STAligner efficiently captures the shared tissue structures across different slices, the disease-related substructures and the dynamical changes during mouse embryonic development. In addition, the shared spatial domain and nearest-neighbor pairs identified by STAligner can be further considered as corresponding pairs to guide the three-dimensional reconstruction of consecutive slices, achieving more accurate local structure-guided registration than the existing method.

In multicellular organisms, cells are deeply influenced by their tissue environment. Knowledge of the spatial position of cells has a critical role in understanding why cells from different locations have distinctive structures and functions. Recent spatial transcriptomics (ST) technologies provide new ways to measure messenger RNA expression and corresponding spatial coordinates in tissues. Various ST technologies have been developed with different spatial resolutions. For example, the spatial barcoding-based method 10x Visium achieves a spatial resolution of 55μm with each capture site containing approximately 1-10 cells. Slide-seq improves spatial resolution to near-cellular level (10μm) and Stereo-seq further reaches subcellular resolution (0.22μm). However, integrative analysis of ST datasets produced by different conditions, technologies or developmental stages remains challenging. Identifying the shared and specific spatial domains (defined as regions with similar spatial expression patterns) is one of the fundamental tasks. STAligner takes the normalized gene expression and spatial coordinates of ST data from multiple tissue slices as input. For each ST slice, STAligner constructs a spatial neighbor graph between spots using the spatial coordinates. STAligner employs a graph attention autoencoder and leverages the graph attention mechanism to learn the spatial heterogeneity.

STAligner defines the triplet that contains anchor-positive and anchor-negative spot pairs. The former is defined as the mutual nearest neighbors (MNNs) with similar gene expressions but belong to two different slices, and the latter belongs to the same slice with different spatial positions and dissimilar expressions. STAligner introduces the triplet loss to update the spot embedding to reduce the distance from the anchor spot to the positive one, and increase the distance from the anchor spot to the negative one. The triplet loss is used to minimize the distance between the anchor-positive pair and maximize the distance between the anchor-negative pair in the latent space z. It is formulated with a Euclidean distance function as follows: $L_{tri}=\frac{1}{N_{tri}}\sum_{(a, p, n)\in\mathcal{C}_{tri}}^{N_{tri}}\max\left(\left\|z_a^{(L)}-z_p^{(L)}\right\|_2-\left\|z_a^{(L)}-z_n^{(L)}\right\|_2+\tau, 0\right)$. The final objective is written as: $L=(1-\alpha) L_{rec}+\alpha L_{tri}$, where $\alpha$ is a hyperparameter that controls the contribution of $L_{rec}$ and $L_{tri}$ in the loss function, and is set by 0.5 by default.

section: Results - Human Cortex & Mouse Olfactory Bulb
text: We applied STAligner to the human dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) ST dataset generated by 10x Visium. It consists of 12 tissue slices from 3 adult samples with 4 adjacent slices for each. By taking the manual annotations as ground truth, we evaluated the performance of STAligner on simultaneous identification of spatial domains and compared it with Harmony, SEDR and PASTE in terms of the adjusted Rand index (ARI). STAligner achieved the highest ARI scores among all methods for the three samples. For example, STAligner accurately captured the layer boundaries and obtained a higher clustering accuracy (ARI=0.61) than Harmony (ARI=0.28), PASTE (ARI=0.36) and SEDR (ARI=0.57) for sample 1. We also applied STAligner to integrate two mouse olfactory bulb ST slices profiled by Slide-seqV2 and Stereo-seq platforms. STAligner can decipher the known tissue structures according to the annotated laminar structure in the DAPI-stained image and Allen Mouse Brain Atlas annotation from both slices, indicating these two platforms indeed capture most of the key tissue structures.

We applied STAligner to integrate four mouse embryo slices sampled at the time stages of E9.5, E10.5, E11.5 and E12.5 profiled by Stereo-seq to investigate the spatiotemporal heterogeneity of tissue structures during mouse organogenesis. Although the four slices have very different sizes and there are obvious batch effects, STAligner can effectively integrate the four slices in the embedding space and align the spatial domains, which showed very consistent localization spatially across the four embryonic stages. We also applied STAligner to two mouse hippocampus tissue slices under normal and Alzheimer's disease (AD) conditions profiled by Slide-seqV2. STAligner could capture shared tissue structures between these two conditions. For example, domains 4, 7 and 11 constructed a clear 'cord-arrow-like' structure in the hippocampal region in both the two slices. We found that domains 0 and 14 were located at the central hippocampal region, but showed clear different compositions between slices, where domain 14 was more enriched in the AD slice.

We demonstrated the capability of automatic registration of seven adjacent mouse hippocampus slices generated by Slide-seqV1+. It is challenging to integrate and register these slices due to the low transcript detection sensitivity of Slide-seqV1 and only partial overlap among these adjacent slices. STAligner could successfully integrate the seven mouse hippocampus slices, and recognize the shared structure of corpus callosum, Ammon's horn and the dentate gyrus. Using the domain 3 as a landmark domain, which has a clear 'cord-arrow-like' structure, the MNNs in this domain identified by STAligner were selected as anchor pairs to guide the registration process using the Iterative Closest Point (ICP) algorithm. All adjacent slices were aligned sequentially with translation and rotation along the z axis. By contrast, PASTE, which was designed for 3D reconstruction of globally similar ST slices, cannot correctly align these slices with partial overlap and thus failed to reconstruct the 3D 'cord-arrow-like' structure. We recorded the computational time and graphics processing unit (GPU) memory usage sets. When integrating the largest mouse hippocampus dataset with seven slices and more than 170,000 spots in total, STAligner is fast and takes only 8.5 min.

The graph attention autoencoder is an unsupervised representation learning framework using both graph structure information and node attributes. It employs the graph attention mechanism to adaptively determine the influence of spatial neighbor nodes on learning node representations and reduce the effect of the noise and missingness in the expression data. Specifically, the output representation of the k-th encoder layer of spot i is formulated as follows: $z_i^{(k)}=\sigma\left(\sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}_i}\alpha_{ij}^{(k)}\left(W^{(k)} z_j^{(k-1)}\right)\right)$, where $\mathcal{N}_{i}$ represents the neighbor set of spot i, $\sigma$ is the nonlinear activation function, $W^{(k)}$ is the trainable weight matrix, $z_{i}^{(0)}$ is the initial gene expression $x_{i}$ of spot i. $\alpha_{ij}^{(k)}$ is set to make the relevance coefficients of spot i's neighbors comparable: $\alpha_{ij}^{(k)}=\frac{\exp\left(e_{ij}^{(k)}\right)}{\sum_{l\in N_{i}} \exp\left(e_{ll}^{(k)}\right)}$, where $e_{ij}^{(k)}$ indicates the relevance of spot i to its neighboring spot j, and can be computed by: $e_{ij}^{(k)}=\operatorname{Sigmoid}\left(v^{s(k)^T}\left(W^{(k)} z_i^{(k-1)}\right)+v^{r(k)^T}\left(W^{(k)} z_j^{(k-1)}\right)\right)$.

The encoder of STAligner consists of two layers with node dimensions set as 512 and 30, respectively, and the decoder is just the reverse of the encoder. The Adam optimizer is used to minimize the loss function with learning rate of $10^{-4}$. We set the weight decay as $10^{-4}$ and the activation function as the exponential linear unit. We adopt the PyTorch Geometric library to implement the graph attention autoencoder. We train the model in two steps: the graph autoencoder model is first trained without the triplet loss (500 iterations by default) and generates the embeddings for triplet identification. Then, we alternate the model training and triplet refinement every 100 iterations until the fixed number of iterations reached (500 iterations by default). The 10x Visium human DLPFC dataset was collected from three independent adult samples. Each sample consists of four adjacent tissue slices. The mouse embryo dataset consisting of four stages (E9.5, E10.5, E11.5 and E12.5) profiled by Stereo-seq, contains 5,913; 18,408; 30,124 and 51,365 spots, respectively.
